ratings.rationale: Common compounds make this approachable, though several prefixes feel interchangeable before the category boundaries settle.
Pure synonym sets with very high-frequency words make the intended groupings almost immediate.
Color-shade groupings are familiar, but a few items like teal and amber introduce slight hesitation over category fit.
Location-based sets are concrete and everyday, with only minor overlap from generally placeable objects.
Straight verb-synonym clustering is clear and uses common vocabulary with little real misdirection.
Terrain-themed items are vivid and familiar, so most groups emerge quickly despite some broad nature overlap.
These adjective-synonym groups are accessible, but several positive-descriptor words could briefly cross-pull between categories.
The continent theme is clear, but accuracy depends on enough geography knowledge and noticing Lagos is a false-looking outlier.
Clothing categories are familiar, though a few fashion terms require some lexical precision to place confidently.
The creative-domain labels are intuitive, but several entries are tools, materials, or concepts rather than the same semantic type.
Weather grouping is natural, but calm and hot categories contain terms that feel more descriptive than event-based.
Animal sounds are highly familiar and the categories are distinct enough to sort almost on sight.
Print-related vocabulary creates strong thematic cohesion, but distinguishing section, type, and format takes an extra pass.
Movement and action synonyms are mostly common, with just enough semantic closeness between run, walk, and escape to slow things slightly.
All the entries live in a cold-themed semantic field, so the main challenge is separating subtype clusters from one another.
Coin-related sets are conceptually tidy, though shared vocabulary around metals and denominations creates mild overlap.
This hinges on spotting four different prefix-compound patterns, with several fragments feeling plausible in more than one slot.
The sound-action synonym groups are familiar and direct, with only light ambiguity at the edges.
The categories are neat once seen, but success depends on fairly specific German food vocabulary and orthographic familiarity.
Film-production terminology is coherent but somewhat specialized, and multiple terms could plausibly belong to adjacent production stages.
Astronomy vocabulary raises the tier through domain knowledge demands and some less transparent technical terms.
This is one of the tougher medium puzzles because it mixes specialized terminology from four games, including several terms many players will not know.
For tech-literate players the sets are fair, but jargon and cross-domain software vocabulary raise the barrier for a typical solver.
The motion-word families are sensible, but the semantic boundaries between turn, bend, twist, and fold are intentionally close.
Chemistry groupings are orderly, yet they rely on remembering periodic-family labels rather than just surface familiarity.
Basic grammar categories are familiar, though mixing affixes with function words adds a little terminology friction.
Literary-period abstraction and theory-heavy vocabulary make this notably hard unless the player already knows the historical schools.
Architectural-style markers are specialized and demand precise art-history knowledge with little help from everyday language.
Rock classification is systematic but quite knowledge-dependent, especially for distinguishing intrusive, extrusive, and clastic terms.
Regional dance and music forms require substantial cultural knowledge, and several unfamiliar names offer limited inference clues.
Human-body system groupings are logical, but the labels are slightly abstract and some organs or structures feel less obviously assigned.
Festival categories are interesting but culturally wide-ranging, with several names likely unfamiliar to a typical player.
Science phenomena are recognizable, though the set mixes processes, effects, and general terms in ways that invite second-guessing.
The repeated 'bar' concept creates strong false trails because every category uses nearby vocabulary from a different sense of the same word.
The four senses of spring are accessible, but some entries are loosely associated rather than exact same-type members.
This leans on four meanings of flat, and several items feel only indirectly connected, making the partitions less clean.
Multiple senses of current produce solid wordplay, but the electrical set in particular blurs related concepts rather than exact equivalents.
The 'scale' theme is clever, though each group mixes objects and associated terms, which makes the category logic feel less immediate.
Different meanings of match are fair, but modern technical language like regex competes with more everyday senses for attention.
The blue-theme sets are evocative yet slippery, with several entries depending on metaphorical association rather than strict category membership.
Metal-based groupings are gettable, but overlap between chemistry and everyday material terms adds some misleading surface similarity.
Bridge as a polysemous anchor works well, but dental and musical vocabulary add enough specialization to make the split tricky.
The word 'cloud' spans technical, meteorological, figurative, and culinary contexts, creating strong thematic overlap and some unconventional items.
Root has four distinct senses, but several groups include adjacent concepts rather than pure examples, which muddies the solve path.
Pitch has well-known multiple meanings, yet the tar sense is less common and some sports entries are region-specific.
The adjective 'sharp' creates nice thematic unity, but the sensory and figurative uses are still common enough to decode without much strain.
Cold branches into temperature, personality, illness, and palette, and the colour set is especially associative rather than exact.
Weight-based metaphorical extensions are clear in hindsight, but abstract senses like legal or emotional weight require more interpretation.
Green spans ecology, envy, inexperience, and money, and the last two categories especially rely on idiomatic understanding.
The four senses of running are familiar, though the operating and political sets depend on a more abstract reading of the word.
Cast has four clean domains and mostly distinct vocabulary, with only modest ambiguity from shared object-oriented nouns.
Wave-based meanings are broad and intuitive, but the social and hair sets are more metaphorical and style-specific.
Light has several common senses, so the puzzle is fair, though the beer category adds a small extra step for non-drinkers.
Key splits into four familiar meanings, and while map and music terminology can blur slightly, the overall structure is approachable.
Ring is a strong multi-meaning anchor and the category vocabulary is mostly recognizable, keeping the challenge moderate.
Draft/draught meanings are distinct, but the spelling variant and mixed noun-process entries create a bit of friction.
Bank as a repeated word sense is easy to grasp, and the surrounding vocabulary makes each domain fairly legible.
Court splits cleanly across legal, royal, tennis, and romance senses, with little serious competition between groups.
Plant taxonomy and common names here are substantially more specialized than average, especially in the fern and moss-lichen groups.
Watch meanings are varied but everyday enough that the four domains usually emerge after a short scan.
